text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 04 June 2024 

Dr Amelia Rowe
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Amelia Rowe
Consultant Neurologist,  

Samuel Carter, DOB: 14-03-1992, Hospital No: S982345 NHS No. 9076543321  
12 Brookfield Close, London, SE15 8QP

Thank you for asking me to review your patient in our neurology clinic. I met with him and his parents today for a scheduled review following recent concerns raised by the family about ongoing events at night and intermittent daytime collapses. There is a remote history of mild traumatic brain injury in late adolescence, without documented intracranial surgery or prolonged loss of consciousness at the time. He completed school and has worked intermittently in retail; he is currently not driving.

Although his seizures fluctuate, his parents consider him reasonably well controlled, he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures multiple nights per week, drop attacks occurring in batches, and tonic seizures during both day and night, this long-standing pattern has persisted without major change. The family keep a paper diary and short video clips on a smartphone; these show stiffening without clear focal onset features on some occasions, and sudden brief losses of tone leading to falls on others. Post-event confusion after the longer nocturnal events is reported to last 20–40 minutes, with morning headache and myalgia. There has been no sustained increase in frequency since the last community review six months ago. No clear triggers have been identified, though sleep restriction appears to worsen nighttime events. He denies alcohol or recreational drug use. 

He remains on a stable regimen of Brivaracetam 100 mg twice daily and Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily, with reported good adherence supported by pharmacy refill records. Rescue midazolam buccal has not been required in the past three months. He has previously tried Levetiracetam and Topiramate, each discontinued due to low mood and cognitive slowing respectively. No recent hospital admissions, head injuries from falls, or tongue lacerations were documented; he wears a protective soft helmet at home during periods when drop attacks cluster. 

On examination today he was alert and cooperative. Speech fluent, no dysarthria. Cranial nerves normal. Tone and power full in all limbs; mild postural tremor noted. Coordination and gait normal between events. No focal neurological deficits were elicited. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations were unremarkable. 

Previous investigations available to me include: routine EEG three years ago showing non-specific intermittent generalised slowing without interictal epileptiform discharges; MRI brain (1.5T) five years ago reported as structurally normal. Blood tests last month (FBC, U&E, LFTs, calcium, magnesium, thyroid profile, vitamin D) were within reference ranges. 

We discussed practical management. The family would like clearer documentation of event types and timing, particularly overnight. I have requested a two-week ambulatory home video-EEG with sleep capture to characterise typical events and to correlate diary/video entries with electrophysiology. He agreed to continue the current antiseizure medicines without changes until we have monitoring results, as the pattern has been stable and there are no new red flags. We also reviewed safety advice at home, including supervised bathing, use of a low bed and padded headboard, and avoiding sleeping on top bunks. He and his parents will continue their seizure diary and share additional videos where feasible. 

Given the remote mild TBI, I have asked radiology to retrieve prior imaging for comparison and will consider a higher-resolution MRI (3T epilepsy protocol) after the ambulatory study if indicated. We also touched on education and employment support; he is interested in a referral to the hospital’s occupational therapy team for fatigue management and workplace planning, which I have arranged. 

I plan to see him again in four months, or sooner if there is a sustained change in pattern, injury, or prolonged recovery after events. The family know how to contact the epilepsy specialist nurse for interim queries. 

Thank you for your referral and ongoing care. Please let me know if there is further information that would be helpful. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures multiple nights per week

Although his seizures fluctuate, his parents consider him reasonably well controlled, he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures multiple nights per week, drop attacks occurring in batches, and tonic seizures during both day and night, this long-standing pattern has persisted without major change

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit frequency statements. The letter states: "he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures multiple nights per week," indicating a vague but clearly weekly frequency for nocturnal GTCs. It also mentions "drop attacks occurring in batches" (clusters) without a clear frequency and "tonic seizures during both day and night" with no numeric frequency. The explicit highest-frequency descriptor provided is "multiple nights per week" for GTCs. No other seizure type has a clearer or higher quantified rate. Therefore, normalize to 'multiple per week' per rules for vague counts.
seizure_frequency_number: he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures multiple nights per week
